title: 4 Example Dataset (The Sandbox)
text:
4.2 Dataset Contents

Raw Data: spatial (and workflow file to generate rasters), formatted climate and observational data
Preprocessing data: Workflow script, all prerequisite files necessary for generating Worldfile/Flowtable, and completed Worldfiles and Flowtables (for a patch, hillslope, and full basin)
Simulation files: Ready-to-run workflow scripts with functions to generate all necessary instruction to run the model for different strategies (i.e., single run, multi-run, for calibration & sensitivity analysis)
Reference outputs: Sample model results
How to get it

title: 1 Introduction (The Model)
text:
1.3 Workflow overview



RHESSys Modeling Workflow Overview



Step 1: Installation - Set up the RHESSys environment
Step 2: Data Preparation - Gather and format spatial, climate, and validation data
Step 3: Preprocessing - Generate the landscape representation (Worldfile) and routing (Flowtable) input files
Step 4: Model Setup and Execution - Define the simulation rules and run RHESSys
Step 5: Calibration & Sensitivity Analysis - Condition the model (Initialize/reach equilibrium), refine parameters, validate performance
Step 6: Post Processing - wrangle, analyze, and visualize output data


Key concepts and terminology
(define frequently used terms)

Spatial hierarchy (basin, hillslope, zone, patch, stratum)
Input File types (Worldfile, Header, Flowtable, Tecfile, Parameter/Climate files, Output filters)
State variables (store quantities) and fluxes
Spatial/Temporal step (i.e., basin daily, patch monthly)

1.3 Workflow overview



RHESSys Modeling Workflow Overview



Step 1: Installation - Set up the RHESSys environment
Step 2: Data Preparation - Gather and format spatial, climate, and validation data
Step 3: Preprocessing - Generate the landscape representation (Worldfile) and routing (Flowtable) input files
Step 4: Model Setup and Execution - Define the simulation rules and run RHESSys
Step 5: Calibration & Sensitivity Analysis - Condition the model (Initialize/reach equilibrium), refine parameters, validate performance
Step 6: Post Processing - wrangle, analyze, and visualize output data


Key concepts and terminology
(define frequently used terms)

Spatial hierarchy (basin, hillslope, zone, patch, stratum)
Input File types (Worldfile, Header, Flowtable, Tecfile, Parameter/Climate files, Output filters)
State variables (store quantities) and fluxes
Spatial/Temporal step (i.e., basin daily, patch monthly)

title: 17 Fine-scale heterogeneity (The Mosaic)
text:
not sure this belongs in advanced features anymore because it has become pretty standard
This can be debated!
I have introduced the term above in Part II because I needed to include preparation of the Aspatial rules file, and about if there are multiple rules a map is needed of rule ID’s, so need to include at least that under spatial data.
Also, I was planning for the example dataset worldfile(s) to use msr, and to discuss that worldfile structure in chapter 6
Perhaps the msr information should be introduced in Part I chapter 3 - Project design & planning? So user knows that as they proceed they will need to make a rules file & possibly a rules map. (maybe in project design introduce standard & msr with info about if you choose this option you need this, if you choose this option you may additionally need this?)
Because that would mean they need to think about the veg makeup of their watershed & perhaps obtain veg maps to base it on?
So either need to stick with standard worldfile structure all the way through and not include anything about msr until advanced topics (include the example worldfiles) - or make msr the standard approach & introduce the topic early on, and it should perhaps have it’s own chapter - or at least it’s own sub-chapter earlier on.
It has quite a bit of information - so i’m leaning toward it’s own chapter - which it has now in advanced topics - but I think it should be introduced earlier
or
MSR could be referred to in project design, and when/why it would be good to use it - with a link to where it lives in advanced topics
Then if user decides to use that option, they go read all about it
This way the topic is introduced so can include reference to it data preparation and preprocessing chapters.
That still leaves the question of whether the example dataset should be standard or msr - or if both are necessary as examples? (perhaps) (but the actual work, with the scripts and the output i think should be done with the msr worldfile)

About Multi-scale routing
How it works

asp rules file
rules map if multiple rules

title: 3 Project Design & Planning (The Strategy)
text:
This chapter will discuss what a user should consider, i.e.:
Please see my comments in the msr documentation where it is placed now - because I think info about msr/asp setup need to be included earlier in the data acquisition & preprocessing sections. Also I think the example dataset should be set up with msr - because it’s easier for a user to “downgrade” their understanding to a standard worldfile than it is to learn the standard and then have to unlearn/retool for MSR later.
Fine-scale Heterogeneity section
To ensure users don’t have to redo their spatial data prep, I think the concept of MSR should be introduced here in Part I chapter 3 (Project Design). This allows the user to decide their model architecture early -
because users will hate getting to the advanced features section and realizing they should have gathered or considered different data earlier. Introducing the MSR/aspatial option here allows them to make an informed architectural decision for their project before they start
The detailed technical execution can remain in Part IV (Advanced Applications) effectively treating Part IV as the ‘Expert Reference Library’ - but I think introduce the concept here, either standard patches or msr patch families and what the difference is - and provide a link to the msr section in advanced topics where they can find the info if they choose that option before moving on to data/setup.

title: 18 Target-driven spin-up (The Alignment)
text:
advanced method of spin-up to reach varying stages of observed ecological states
run to reach spatially explicit targets, i.e. LAI
types of spin-up targets
sources for deriving targets
Prerequisites to running:
spin-up target file
target file header
spin-up definition file
worldfile with spinup object ID
worldfile header pointer
command line options

title: 5 User-Acquired Data (The Ingredients)
text:
Required

DEM (source to obtain)
Watershed outlet - gauge location coordinates

DEM Derivatives - generation discussed in later section:

Watershed Basin boundary
Sub-basins (hillslopes)
Slope/Aspect
E/W Horizons
Stream Network
Patches



Recommended (typical) or when applicable:

Vegetation cover
Soils
Landcover/Landuse
Roads
Impervious areas
Climate base stations
Aspatial rules ID map for multi-scale routing

title: 5 User-Acquired Data (The Ingredients)
text:
5.3 Observational Data

used to constrain and evaluate hydrologic and ecophysiological parameterization
Hydrologic (i.e., Streamflow, Snow, Soil moisture)
Vegetation (i.e, LAI, Height, NPP, ET, rooting depth)

title: 13 Validation (The Confirmation)
text:
judging/evaluation performance
testing calibrated parameters against data set independent of calibration period

title: 16 Disturbance: Vegetation Dynamics (The Evolution)
text:
Land Cover/Vegetation change
biomass pool changes
redefine world functionality (redefine_world_: multiplier, thin_remain, thin_harvest, thin_snags, thin_harvest) to invoke:

drought induced mortality
fuel treatments

thinning, controlled burns


maybe include redefine world info on urbanization?
